codeReview.summary: The implementation does not satisfy the feature scope: it still relies on post-commit cleanup deletes after hydrate failures rather than making save/save_many all-or-nothing at the commit boundary itself.

issues.description: Repo::save commits the UPSERT transaction before calling hydrate_foreign, then tries to repair failures with db.delete on the parent and tracked foreign rows. That is explicitly the forbidden post-commit compensating-delete design, so the commit boundary is not all-or-nothing as required.

Repo::save_many likewise commits the batch before hydrate_foreign runs, and on failure it issues best-effort deletes for parents and tracked children. This leaves atomicity dependent on cleanup succeeding after commit instead of on the transaction boundary itself.

The new regressions only assert the database is clean after the compensating deletes complete. They do not prove the required stronger contract that the public error path never exposes already-committed residue without relying on post-commit cleanup.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker reported the feature as already satisfying a true atomic boundary even though the reviewed commit still uses post-commit delete cleanup in save/save_many. The skill/procedure may need a sharper check that commit-boundary atomicity is evidenced in code, not inferred from passing residue-cleanup tests.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff for session d1c8b447-4b83-4dd1-ab00-33e0241b160a says save/save_many rely on a true transaction boundary, but commit 491c889 shows deletes after COMMIT in core/src/repository/mod.rs:574-589 and 704-728.

summary: Fail: commit 491c889 improves cleanup after failed hydrations, but it does not replace the forbidden post-commit compensating-delete approach with a true all-or-nothing save/save_many boundary.
